generated_code:
#include<iostream>
 
void optimize(std::istream& in)
{
    // Disable synchronization between cin and stdin to improve performance on Windows systems with Visual Studio compiler. This is not necessary when using other compilers or operating systems such as Linux/macOS where this optimization does nothing anyway!
#ifdef _MSC_VER    
#pragma warning(disable : 4996)//warning about deprecated function 'std::_Read_narrow_s' being used by Microsoft's implementation of standard library functions like getc(), getchar()
#endif
 
#if defined(_WIN32)||defined(__CYGWIN__)||!__GNUC__
	in.sync_with_stdio(true);//Disable syncronization between input stream(in here it means STDIN_FILENO which points at console window )and underlying OS file descriptor(STDOUT_FILENO).This will reduce overhead caused due IO operations performed over network sockets etc...
#elseif __cplusplus >= 201703L || (__STDC_VERSION__>=C_STD_2018&& !defined(__clang__))
	stdin->_IO_read_ptr=stdin->buf;//Set read pointer position backto beginningof buffer sothat next (e.g., reading gigabytes worth offilecontent).
#elif !(defined(WINVER)&& WINVER<=5)&!(defined(_WINNT_WINVERSION)&& (_WINNT_WINVERSION<WindowsXP))&&(defined(__MINGW32_MAJOR)>=4||(defined(__MINGW_MINOR)>=(sizeof(unsigned int)*CHAR_BIT-((unsigned char)'a')*INT_MAX)))
	setvbuf(stdin,NULL,_IONBF,sizeof(char));//Use un-buffered I/O mode instead buffered one because latter requires additional memory allocation/deallocation steps during each operation making overall execution slower compared against former approach especially under low-memory conditions common among embedded devices running resource constrained environments without enough RAM availablefor storing large amounts data temporarily while processing them sequentially through multiple stages within application logic flowchart diagrammed below:https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/what-is-the-difference-between-unbuffered-and-line-oriented-input-output-in-c/
 #endif 
 
}
